title: Bootstrap Anchor, frontend, and automation workspace
description:
Create the minimum runnable workspace needed to implement Meridian across program, frontend, and automation packages.

Scope:
- Establish the Anchor workspace and package layout.
- Add frontend and automation package skeletons with shared config boundaries.
- Define environment variable surfaces and add .env.example.
- Add reproducible local commands for build, test, and devnet deployment.
- Keep the setup minimal and implementation-oriented.

Done when:
- A new developer can install dependencies and run the core local commands.
- Program, frontend, and automation code have a stable home in the repo.
- The repo is ready for implementation work without structural churn.
close_reason: Completed

title: Story: implement Pyth price adapter and validation rules
description:
Implement the settlement-data path for Meridian using Pyth.

Acceptance criteria:
- The program or integration layer can read the agreed regular-session settlement update.
- Staleness and confidence checks are enforced consistently.
- Invalid or missing price updates fail cleanly before settlement mutation.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing validation tests first for valid data, stale data, wrong feed, and wide-confidence rejection.
notes:
Primary validation tests for this story:
- Valid regular-session settlement update is accepted.
- Stale update is rejected.
- Wrong feed or mismatched ticker/feed mapping is rejected.
- Wide-confidence update is rejected before settlement mutation.
- Settlement read follows the agreed last regular-session update at or before 4:00 PM ET rule.
close_reason: Already implemented: oracle/pyth.rs validation (feed match, staleness, confidence, zero-price), domain/oracle/pyth.ts mirroring, and full test coverage in pyth.test.ts

title: Story: implement Add Strike admin instruction
description:
Implement the admin-only instruction that adds extra strikes to an existing stock's market set intraday.

Acceptance criteria:
- Admin can add a new strike for a stock that already has markets for the current trading day.
- The new strike creates a full market (market account, vault, Yes mint, No mint, Phoenix market) identical to morning-created markets.
- Duplicate strike for the same stock/day is rejected.
- Non-admin callers are rejected.
- The instruction is only callable while the protocol is active and before market close.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing program tests for successful add-strike, duplicate rejection, non-admin rejection, and post-close rejection before implementation.
close_reason: Implemented add_strike admin instruction with Clock-based post-close rejection, admin_authority constraint, shared initialize_market_fields helper, and usdc_mint validation

title: Phase 4: complete program crate structure split
description:
Purpose:
Complete the program-crate refactor beyond state so the Anchor program has clear homes for instruction logic, account types, oracle integration, and Phoenix integration without changing protocol behavior.

Files this phase should touch:
- programs/meridian/src/lib.rs
- programs/meridian/src/instructions/**
- programs/meridian/src/accounts/**
- programs/meridian/src/oracle/**
- programs/meridian/src/phoenix/**
- programs/meridian/src/constants.rs only if necessary to support cleaner module boundaries

Files this phase should avoid touching:
- programs/meridian/src/state/** unless a tiny import/wiring fix is unavoidable
- automation/**
- app/**
- packages/domain/**
- tests/** except where existing program/integration tests must be updated to keep behavior green

How to accomplish it:
1. Keep lib.rs narrow: program entrypoints, exports, and macro-sensitive Anchor wiring only.
2. Move non-macro business logic into instruction modules.
3. Keep Anchor account structs at crate root when the macros require it; do not fight the macro system for aesthetic purity.
4. Use accounts/* for reusable account aliases or account-surface helpers where that actually reduces duplication.
5. Use oracle/* for Pyth-specific logic and phoenix/* for Phoenix-specific logic or stubs.
6. Preserve current behavior; do not implement new protocol features in this phase beyond what is required to stabilize structure.

Verification required:
- cargo test --manifest-path programs/meridian/Cargo.toml
- pnpm test
- If public exports move, confirm the existing Rust and workspace tests still pass unchanged

Done when:
- The program crate has stable homes for instructions, accounts, oracle, and Phoenix code.
- lib.rs is not accumulating business logic.
- Existing behavior and tests remain green.
close_reason: Completed

title: Story: implement landing page with product explanation and connect-wallet CTA
description:
Implement the landing page as the first user-facing entry point to Meridian.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Landing page renders product explanation, supported tickers, and connect-wallet CTA.
2. Live market prices or contract counts are visible before wallet connection.
3. Wallet connection from the landing page transitions to the markets view.
4. Page is accessible at the app root route.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing frontend behavior tests for page rendering, pre-connection data display, and wallet-connect transition.

title: Add merge primitive and define Sell No close path
description:
Make merge a first-class protocol primitive so Sell No has a clean pre-settlement close path.

Scope:
- Add merge to the required contract surface.
- Define merge semantics: burn equal Yes and No, return equal USDC from the vault, pre-settlement only.
- Specify account checks, authority rules, and invariant preservation.
- Define the baseline Sell No flow as buy Yes then merge.
- Treat one-click atomic Sell No as a later Phoenix composition task, not a prerequisite for protocol correctness.

Done when:
- Merge is part of the implementation plan and test plan.
- Sell No is no longer a protocol gap.
- The contract and frontend can target a correct two-step flow before atomic UX work.
close_reason: Documented lifecycle and merge decisions in requirements and research docs

title: Story: bootstrap Phoenix markets and seat workflow
description:
Implement the Phoenix-side market bootstrap needed for Meridian trading.

Acceptance criteria:
- A Yes/USDC market can be created or configured for a Meridian strike.
- Required seat approval and authority steps are documented in code and config.
- The integration boundary between our program and Phoenix is explicit.

TDD starting point:
- Start with failing integration tests or harness checks for market bootstrap success and missing-seat failure cases.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A custom Yes/USDC Phoenix market can be bootstrapped on devnet for a Meridian strike.
2. Seat request and approval are exercised explicitly, either via the Phoenix Seat Manager program or the configured market authority path.
3. Bootstrap config documents and validates the Phoenix program ID, Seat Manager program ID, zero taker fee policy, and market authority mode.
4. Bootstrap tests cover missing-seat or missing-seat-manager failures, and Meridian orders are configured to expire at or before market close.
notes:
Primary integration tests for this story:
- Custom Yes/USDC Phoenix market bootstrap succeeds on devnet for a Meridian strike.
- Seat request and approval workflow succeeds.
- Missing-seat or missing-seat-manager paths fail clearly.
- Bootstrap config enforces the required market authority and order-expiry-at-or-before-close assumptions.
close_reason: Completed: Phoenix program cloned into localnet, Phoenix client + seat workflow implemented, integration tests added, create-markets job wired up, bootstrap-check enhanced

title: Phase 5: complete frontend feature/module bootstrap
description:
Purpose:
Complete the frontend feature bootstrap so route files stay thin and the app code is organized by product surface: wallet, markets, trading, and portfolio.

Files this phase should touch:
- app/src/app/**
- app/src/components/**
- app/src/features/**
- app/src/lib/**
- app/src/hooks/**
- app/package.json only if needed for dependency alignment

Files this phase should avoid touching:
- automation/**
- programs/**
- packages/domain/** except for consuming its exported types/constants
- tests/** except for frontend-facing tests if they are added under the new layout later

How to accomplish it:
1. Keep route files in app/src/app/* as composition shells only.
2. Move reusable UI or view-model logic into app/src/features/*.
3. Keep shared presentational pieces in app/src/components/*.
4. Keep Solana/network/environment helpers in app/src/lib/*.
5. Do not introduce a heavyweight global state solution unless the existing code clearly demands it.
6. Preserve current behavior and current routes; this phase is about structure, not feature expansion.

Verification required:
- pnpm --filter @meridian/app typecheck
- pnpm --filter @meridian/app build
- pnpm test
- If route wiring changes, confirm the root page still builds and renders as a static route

Done when:
- The app has stable feature folders for wallet, markets, trading, and portfolio.
- Route files are thin and reusable code no longer has to live under app/src/app directly.
- Existing build behavior remains green.
close_reason: Completed

title: Story: initialize program config and authority model
description:
Implement the one-time config path for Meridian.

Acceptance criteria:
- A config account can be initialized exactly once per deployment scope.
- Supported ticker/oracle references and admin authority are stored deterministically.
- Re-initialization and invalid authority cases fail cleanly.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing program tests for successful init, double-init rejection, and wrong-authority rejection before implementation.
close_reason: Completed: initialize_config instruction with deterministic ticker mapping and rejection tests

title: Story: implement post-settlement redemption flows
description:
Implement winner redemption after market resolution.

Acceptance criteria:
- Winning holders can redeem and receive correct USDC.
- Losing holders cannot extract collateral.
- Partial redemption and repeated redemption attempts behave correctly.
- Redemption is only available after settlement.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing tests for winning redemption, losing-token rejection, partial redemption math, and double-redemption rejection.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Winning holders can redeem and receive correct USDC.
2. Losing holders cannot extract collateral.
3. Partial redemption and repeated redemption attempts behave correctly.
4. Redemption is only available after settlement.
5. Multi-user redemption test: after two users trade against each other and the market settles, both redeem their winning positions correctly and vault balance reaches zero.
notes:
Primary program tests for this story:
- Winning holder redeems and receives correct USDC.
- Losing-token redemption is rejected.
- Partial redemption math is correct.
- Repeated or double redemption attempts are rejected.
- Multi-user integration test (requirements.md): one user mints and quotes, another takes, market settles, both redeem. Extend the existing trade-yes.test.ts two-actor setup with settlement + dual-redemption assertions.
close_reason: Completed

title: Story: implement the layered test harness
description:
Implement the test structure that supports contract-first and behavior-first development.

Acceptance criteria:
- The repo has clear homes for contract, integration, automation, and frontend tests.
- Core commands for running targeted and full test suites are defined.
- Test fixtures and fakes are usable enough to drive development before full devnet integration.

TDD starting point:
- Start by adding one failing test in each layer and wiring the commands that make those tests runnable.
notes:
Harness should support these initial layers and example first tests:
- Program/integration: create market, mint pair, merge pair, settlement, redemption.
- Phoenix integration: market bootstrap/seat workflow, direct Yes-side trading, No-side composition.
- Automation: strike generation unit tests, morning job orchestration, settlement job orchestration.
- Frontend behavior: wallet/discovery, trade intent mapping, portfolio/redeem visibility.
- Devnet smoke: narrow end-to-end create -> mint -> trade -> settle -> redeem path.
close_reason: Added 6 targeted test scripts, created frontend and smoke test homes with todo stubs

title: Story: support mint, merge, and pause with invariant checks
description:
Implement the core pre-settlement token lifecycle on-chain.

Acceptance criteria:
- Users can mint a pair by depositing exactly $1 USDC per pair.
- Users can merge equal Yes and No amounts pre-settlement and receive matching USDC back.
- Pause state blocks the intended actions cleanly.
- Vault balance and token-supply invariants hold across mint and merge paths.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing invariant tests first for mint math, merge math, pause enforcement, and invalid-balance rejection.
notes:
Primary integration tests for this story:
- Mint pair deposits exactly $1 USDC per pair and preserves vault collateralization.
- Merge pair burns equal Yes/No amounts pre-settlement and returns matching USDC.
- Pause state rejects the intended actions cleanly.
- Invalid balance or supply states are rejected without violating invariants.
close_reason: Implemented mint_pair, merge_pair, pause_protocol, unpause_protocol instructions with SPL Token CPI, invariant checks, market-config binding, and pause enforcement

title: Story: implement composed No-side trading flows
description:
Implement Buy No and Sell No using the agreed composition rules.

Acceptance criteria:
- Buy No works via mint-and-sell-Yes composition.
- Sell No works via buy-Yes then merge.
- The baseline flows are correct even before one-click atomic optimization exists.
- Resulting balances and collateral effects remain correct across the composed paths.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing composition tests first for Buy No and Sell No, including balance math and failure rollback cases.
notes:
Baseline composition remains fixed: Buy No is mint-and-sell-Yes, and Sell No is buy-Yes then merge. One-click atomic composition is a later optimization after direct Yes-side trading and Phoenix bootstrap are stable.

Primary composition tests for this story:
- Buy No via mint pair then sell Yes, keeping No.
- Sell No via buy Yes then merge.
- Balance math and collateral effects remain correct across both flows.
- Failure rollback leaves the user and vault in a consistent state.
close_reason: Integration tests prove Buy No and Sell No composition via mint+sell-yes and buy-yes+merge

title: Core feature epic: daily automation and market operations
description:
Deliver the off-chain operating layer that creates markets each morning and settles them each afternoon.

Scope:
- Generate strikes from the previous close and handle deduplication.
- Create daily markets idempotently across supported tickers.
- Run the settlement job with retries and alerting hooks.
- Handle failure paths and manual override escalation.
- Manage operational configuration for schedules, feed mappings, and thresholds.

Done when:
- Daily market creation and settlement can run reproducibly on devnet.
- Operational failure paths are explicit and recoverable.
- The system no longer depends on manual step-by-step operator intervention for the normal path.

title: Meridian MVP roadmap: core feature epics
description:
Goal: define the high-level implementation plan for Meridian as a set of core feature epics.

This roadmap starts after the foundation work in br-guy and organizes implementation around product capabilities rather than setup tasks.

Execution order:
1. On-chain market engine
2. Phoenix trading flows
3. Oracle settlement and redemption
4. Daily automation and market operations
5. Trader-facing frontend experience
6. Devnet deployment, testing, and demo readiness

Done when:
- Each core feature has a dedicated epic with a clear product boundary.
- The roadmap is implementation-oriented and sequenced for delivery.
- Follow-on tasks can be attached under the relevant feature epic instead of accumulating under one generic plan.

title: Freeze market lifecycle and settlement semantics
description:
Define the simplest explicit market lifecycle for Meridian and remove ambiguity from the requirements.

Scope:
- Formalize 4:00 PM ET market close behavior.
- Decide whether minting, new orders, and cancellations are allowed after 4:00 PM ET.
- Define how Phoenix resting orders expire and whether market status is switched explicitly.
- Lock the settlement timing rule, retry window, and admin override delay.
- Produce one canonical lifecycle/state-transition table for create -> active -> closed -> settled -> redeemable.

Done when:
- The lifecycle is written down in implementation-ready terms.
- There is no remaining ambiguity about what the protocol and automation service do at and after 4:00 PM ET.
- Follow-on contract and automation work can treat these rules as fixed.
close_reason: Documented lifecycle and merge decisions in requirements and research docs

title: Define strike generation, automation boundaries, and failure paths
description:
Turn the daily lifecycle requirements into deterministic automation behavior.

Scope:
- Define strike-generation rules and deduplication behavior.
- Define idempotent morning market creation behavior.
- Define settlement job behavior, retries, and manual override handoff.
- Define config surfaces for market hours, ticker mapping, and operational thresholds.
- Clarify what the automation service owns versus what the program enforces.

Done when:
- Morning and settlement jobs have stable boundaries.
- Failure paths are explicit rather than implicit.
- Automation work can start without reopening product-level questions.
close_reason: done

title: Story: implement wallet and market discovery views
description:
Implement the first user-facing entry path into Meridian.

Acceptance criteria:
- A user can connect a wallet and see connection state clearly.
- The markets view lists active markets with enough data to choose where to trade.
- Empty, loading, and disconnected states are handled cleanly.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing frontend behavior tests first for wallet state transitions, market-list rendering, and loading/empty states.
notes:
Primary frontend behavior tests for this story:
- Wallet connection and disconnection state transitions.
- Market discovery list renders active markets with required summary data.
- Loading, empty, and disconnected states behave cleanly.
- The view tolerates markets not yet tradeable without breaking navigation.
close_reason: Completed

title: Core feature epic: oracle settlement and redemption
description:
Deliver the close-of-day resolution path that turns live markets into settled outcomes and pays winners correctly.

Scope:
- Read the settlement price from Pyth using the agreed regular-session rule.
- Validate staleness and confidence thresholds.
- Resolve each market outcome immutably.
- Support admin override settlement under the defined delay and emergency rules.
- Support post-settlement redemption of winning tokens.

Done when:
- Markets can be settled correctly on devnet.
- Winning token holders can redeem USDC end to end.
- Settlement logic is testable across above, below, and at-strike cases.

title: Story: implement settlement job with retries and escalation
description:
Implement the afternoon automation path that settles markets or escalates correctly.

Acceptance criteria:
- The job attempts settlement after market close using the agreed retry policy.
- Stale or low-confidence oracle conditions trigger retries instead of bad settlement writes.
- Failed settlement exits with a clear escalation path for admin override.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing orchestration tests first for happy-path settlement, retry-until-success, and retry-exhausted escalation.
notes:
Primary orchestration tests for this story:
- Happy-path settlement job settles open markets after close.
- Stale or low-confidence oracle conditions trigger retry-until-success behavior.
- Retry exhaustion produces a clear escalation path for admin override.
- No invalid settlement writes occur during retry handling.

title: Story: enforce position constraints in trading UI
description:
Implement frontend-level enforcement that prevents users from holding both Yes and No tokens for the same strike from trading.

Acceptance criteria:
- Before presenting trade options, the UI checks the user's token balances for the selected strike.
- If the user holds No tokens, Buy Yes is disabled with guidance to sell No first.
- If the user holds Yes tokens, Buy No is disabled with guidance to sell Yes first.
- The constraint is checked on every trade panel render and after trade execution.
- Transient dual-holding during mint-pair operations is allowed (not blocked).

TDD starting point:
- Write failing frontend behavior tests for: holds-No-blocks-Buy-Yes, holds-Yes-blocks-Buy-No, zero-balance-allows-both, and post-trade-recheck before implementation.

title: Core feature epic: Phoenix trading and four trade flows
description:
Deliver the trading layer on top of Meridian markets using Phoenix as the CLOB.

Scope:
- Create and manage Yes/USDC Phoenix markets for each strike.
- Support Buy Yes and Sell Yes directly on the book.
- Support Buy No via mint-and-sell-Yes composition.
- Support Sell No via buy-Yes then merge, with atomic composition as a later enhancement.
- Expose order-book views that support both Yes and No perspectives.

Done when:
- All four trade paths are supported by correct protocol and integration flows.
- Phoenix market creation and order placement are stable on devnet.
- Trading behavior can feed the frontend without architectural rework.
notes: Phoenix bootstrap assumptions are now fixed for follow-on work: custom Yes/USDC markets on devnet, Phoenix Seat Manager path available, zero Phoenix taker fees for V1, SPL Token-only assets, and order expiry at or before Meridian market close.

title: Story: implement the end-to-end devnet demo path
description:
Implement the reproducible happy-path proof for Meridian on devnet.

Acceptance criteria:
- The team can run a scripted or documented create -> mint -> trade -> settle -> redeem flow.
- The demo path is narrow, explicit, and stable enough for review.
- Failures in the demo path are diagnosable without tribal knowledge.

TDD starting point:
- Start with a failing smoke path definition, then make each stage go green one at a time.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The team can run a scripted or documented create -> Phoenix bootstrap and seat approval -> mint -> trade -> settle -> redeem flow.
2. The demo path is narrow, explicit, and stable enough for review.
3. Failures in the demo path are diagnosable without tribal knowledge.
notes:
Target narrow E2E smoke paths:
1. Create market -> mint pair -> buy yes -> settle yes-wins -> redeem.
2. Create market -> mint pair -> buy no -> settle no-wins -> redeem.
3. Hold No -> buy Yes -> merge to realize Sell No close path before settlement.
4. Morning automation creates markets deterministically -> afternoon automation settles them using the agreed retry/escalation policy.

title: Story: create strike markets with vaults and token mints
description:
Implement the contract path that creates one stock/strike/day market.

Acceptance criteria:
- Market creation allocates the market account, vault, Yes mint, and No mint.
- PDA seeds and addresses are deterministic and collision-safe.
- Duplicate market creation for the same stock/strike/day is rejected.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing tests for successful market creation, duplicate rejection, and correct account initialization before implementation.
notes:
Primary integration tests for this story:
- Successful market creation allocates the market account, vault, Yes mint, and No mint.
- Duplicate creation for the same stock/strike/day is rejected.
- PDA seeds and initialized account metadata are deterministic and collision-safe.
- Market bootstrap data is sufficient for downstream Phoenix and automation stories.
close_reason: Implemented create_market instruction with deterministic PDA seeds, vault/yes_mint/no_mint init, duplicate rejection via Anchor init, and usdc_mint validation against config

title: Phase 3: complete automation package structure split
description:
Purpose:
Complete the automation-structure refactor so the automation package has clear boundaries between configuration, pure domain logic, network clients, commands, jobs, and orchestration.

Files this phase should touch:
- automation/src/index.ts
- automation/src/commands/**
- automation/src/config/**
- automation/src/clients/**
- automation/src/domain/**
- automation/src/jobs/**
- automation/src/orchestration/**
- automation/package.json if required for script or dependency alignment
- package.json only if root automation wiring must be updated

Files this phase should avoid touching:
- programs/meridian/src/**
- app/src/**
- packages/domain/** except for narrowly-scoped imports already exposed
- tests/** except when a new automation-specific test must be added under the new layered structure

How to accomplish it:
1. Keep automation/src/index.ts as a thin entrypoint only.
2. Move bootstrap command behavior into automation/src/commands/bootstrap-check.ts or equivalent command modules.
3. Separate env parsing from derived runtime state in automation/src/config/{env,runtime}.ts.
4. Keep HTTP/RPC/Phoenix integrations in automation/src/clients/*.
5. Keep pure business logic in automation/src/domain/*.
6. Create explicit job entrypoints for create-markets and settle-markets even if they begin as shells.
7. Do not introduce generic utils/services buckets.
8. Preserve current behavior; this phase is structural, not a feature expansion.

Verification required:
- pnpm --filter @meridian/automation typecheck
- pnpm --filter @meridian/automation build
- pnpm test
- If root scripts are touched, re-run the affected root command and confirm no regression

Done when:
- The automation package has stable directories for commands, config, clients, domain, jobs, and orchestration.
- The current bootstrap path still works through the new structure.
- No app/program files were modified outside the declared surface.
close_reason: Completed

title: Core feature epic: on-chain market engine
description:
Build the on-chain Meridian program capabilities that define a market and manage collateral before trading and settlement concerns.

Scope:
- Initialize global config and authority surfaces.
- Create strike markets with Yes/No mints and collateral vaults.
- Support mint pair and merge pair flows.
- Define pause controls and core state transitions.
- Enforce the non-negotiable collateral and token invariants on-chain.

Done when:
- A market can be created deterministically on devnet.
- Users can mint and merge pairs correctly.
- The program state model is stable enough for trading and settlement integration.

title: Story: implement morning market creation job
description:
Implement the morning automation path that creates daily markets.

Acceptance criteria:
- The job creates all required markets for the day idempotently.
- Re-runs do not create duplicate markets.
- Failures are surfaced with enough context for recovery.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing job tests first for fresh-run success, idempotent re-run behavior, and partial-failure reporting.
notes:
Primary orchestration tests for this story:
- Fresh-run morning job creates the required markets for the day.
- Re-running the same job is idempotent and does not create duplicates.
- Partial-failure cases surface enough context for recovery and rerun.
- Successfully created markets are not recreated on retry.
close_reason: Completed

title: Core feature epic: trader-facing frontend experience
description:
Deliver the user-facing application for discovery, trading, portfolio management, and redemption.

Scope:
- Landing and markets views with wallet connection and live market discovery.
- Trade view with Yes/No perspectives over one Phoenix order book.
- Position-aware trade controls for Buy Yes, Buy No, Sell Yes, and Sell No.
- Portfolio and history views with positions, outcomes, and redeem actions.
- Clear presentation of countdowns, prices, and payout semantics.

Done when:
- A user can navigate from discovery to trade to portfolio to redemption in one coherent app flow.
- The frontend correctly abstracts the one-book / two-perspective trading model.
- Wallet signing and transaction status flows are stable enough for demo use.

title: Story: implement direct Yes-side trading flows
description:
Implement Buy Yes and Sell Yes through Phoenix.

Acceptance criteria:
- Users can place Buy Yes and Sell Yes orders against the Yes/USDC book.
- Order placement, fill handling, and resulting balances are correct.
- Post-close or paused-state trading attempts fail cleanly.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing integration tests for Buy Yes and Sell Yes happy paths, plus rejection tests for post-close or paused execution.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Users can place Buy Yes and Sell Yes orders against the Yes/USDC book.
2. Order placement requires an approved Phoenix seat and uses order packets that expire at or before market close.
3. Fill handling and resulting balances are correct.
4. Post-close, paused-state, or missing-seat trading attempts fail cleanly.
notes:
Primary integration tests for this story:
- Buy Yes happy path through the Yes/USDC book.
- Sell Yes happy path through the Yes/USDC book.
- Fill handling and resulting balances are correct.
- Post-close, paused-state, or missing-seat trading attempts fail cleanly.
close_reason: done

title: Spike Phoenix integration for market creation and trading flows
description:
Reduce Phoenix integration risk before committing to full implementation.

Scope:
- Validate custom Yes/USDC market creation on devnet.
- Validate seat approval and market authority workflow.
- Prove the basic order-placement path we need for Meridian.
- Identify the exact path for later atomic Buy No and Sell No composition.
- Capture any Phoenix-specific constraints that affect account design or UX.

Done when:
- Phoenix risks are reduced to concrete implementation tasks.
- We know the exact integration boundaries between the Anchor program, automation, and frontend.
- There are no major unknowns left about market creation or seat handling.
close_reason: Implemented Phoenix bootstrap config, validation tests, README boundary, and downstream issue updates

title: Core feature epic: devnet deployment, testing, and demo readiness
description:
Deliver the proof that Meridian works end to end on devnet and can be reproduced by another developer or reviewer.

Scope:
- Reproducible setup, build, and deployment commands.
- Program, integration, and frontend test coverage for the MVP path.
- End-to-end devnet flow for create -> mint -> trade -> settle -> redeem.
- Demo-oriented scripts and environment configuration.
- Clear README-level operating instructions for running the system locally and on devnet.

Done when:
- Another developer can stand up the system and reproduce the core lifecycle.
- The project has a realistic quality bar for shipping the MVP demo.
- Demo readiness is no longer implicit or manual.

title: Story: implement settlement and override instructions
description:
Implement the state transition from live market to settled market.

Acceptance criteria:
- Settlement writes an immutable outcome using validated oracle data.
- Override settlement is gated by the agreed delay and authority checks.
- Double-settlement and invalid transition attempts fail cleanly.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing program tests for above-strike, below-strike, at-strike, double-settle rejection, and premature override rejection.
notes:
Primary program tests for this story:
- Above-strike, below-strike, and exactly-at-strike settlement outcomes.
- Double-settlement rejection.
- Premature override rejection before the enforced delay.
- Invalid transition or premature settlement attempts fail cleanly after market close rules are applied.

title: Story: implement market close automation sequence
description:
Implement the 4:00 PM ET market close automation that halts trading and transitions markets to Closed state.

Acceptance criteria:
- At 4:00 PM ET, the automation service sets each active Phoenix market status to Closed.
- Minting is halted by transitioning each MeridianMarket to the Closed phase.
- All Phoenix orders have already expired via TTL (orders must be submitted with expiry at or before 4:00 PM ET).
- No new orders or cancellations are accepted after market close.
- The close sequence runs before the settlement job and is idempotent on re-run.
- Failures are surfaced with enough context for recovery.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing orchestration tests for successful close sequence, idempotent re-run, and partial-failure reporting before implementation.

title: Story: implement Phoenix order book data pipeline for frontend
description:
Implement the real-time data pipeline that connects the frontend to Phoenix order book state.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Frontend can subscribe to a Phoenix market account and receive real-time order book updates.
2. The book is parsed into a UI-ready bid/ask ladder for the Yes perspective.
3. The No perspective is derived by inverting the Yes book ($1.00 - price).
4. Stale or disconnected subscription state is surfaced to the consumer.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing tests for Yes ladder parsing, No-side price inversion, and subscription disconnect handling.

title: Story: implement portfolio, history, and redeem views
description:
Implement the post-trade user experience.

Acceptance criteria:
- Users can see active positions, settled outcomes, and redeem actions.
- History view shows prior activity clearly enough for debugging and demo use.
- Portfolio math and redeem visibility stay in sync with backend state.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing frontend tests first for portfolio rendering, redeem-button visibility, and history-state presentation.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Portfolio renders active positions and settled outcomes correctly.
2. Redeem button visibility matches backend settlement and ownership state.
3. Portfolio math remains in sync with trade and redemption events.
4. Users who have minted pairs and posted limit orders can see their exposure and fill state (market maker visibility).
5. History view presents a trade execution log with transaction-level data (fills, timestamps, prices) sourced from Phoenix transaction signatures, not just portfolio position state.
6. History is accessible as a distinct view or tab, not buried inside the portfolio summary.
notes:
Primary frontend behavior tests for this story:
- Portfolio renders active positions and settled outcomes correctly.
- Redeem button visibility matches backend settlement and ownership state.
- History view presents prior activity clearly enough for debugging and demo use.
- Portfolio math remains in sync with trade and redemption events.
- Users who have minted pairs and posted limit orders can see their exposure and fill state in the portfolio view.
- Trade execution log uses Phoenix transaction signature events (getPhoenixEventsFromTransactionSignature or equivalent) as the data source, not just derived position state.
- Market maker flow (mint & quote) exposure is visible in the portfolio — requirements.md explicitly lists this as a user story.

title: Phase 6: finalize workspace hygiene and architecture docs
description:
Purpose:
Complete the final workspace hygiene and documentation pass after the structural refactors have landed so scripts, docs, and ignore rules match the actual repo shape.

Files this phase should touch:
- package.json
- pnpm-workspace.yaml only if package discovery needs final cleanup
- README.md
- .gitignore
- architecture.md if added
- Any other root-level documentation files that explain commands or layout

Files this phase should avoid touching:
- app/src/** except if a command or doc example must be updated to match a renamed path
- automation/src/** except for command name alignment
- programs/meridian/src/** except for command/doc references
- packages/domain/** and packages/testkit/** except for documenting them

How to accomplish it:
1. Wait until the automation, program, and frontend structure phases are functionally complete.
2. Align root scripts with the final package layout and test/build commands.
3. Tighten ignore rules for generated artifacts and build output.
4. Update README.md to describe the current package/module structure rather than the original scaffold only.
5. Add a short architecture.md that explains layer ownership: protocol, shared domain, automation, frontend, and testkit.
6. Keep this phase documentation-oriented; do not sneak feature work into it.

Verification required:
- pnpm test
- pnpm typecheck
- pnpm build
- Manually verify that README/architecture docs match the actual repo structure and commands

Done when:
- Root scripts reflect the final structure.
- Docs describe the current repo, not the old scaffold.
- Ignore rules make generated artifacts clearly disposable.
- The whole workspace still passes test, typecheck, and build.
close_reason: Already complete: root scripts reflect final structure, README describes current repo, .gitignore covers generated artifacts, workspace passes test/typecheck/build

title: Spike Pyth settlement and feed handling on Solana devnet
description:
Reduce Pyth integration risk for settlement and daily market operations.

Scope:
- Verify the devnet feed configuration for all supported tickers.
- Prove the pull-oracle update flow needed for settlement.
- Define staleness and confidence checks in implementation-ready terms.
- Confirm the simplest settlement read rule for the regular session.
- Capture any SDK or Anchor compatibility constraints that affect implementation.

Done when:
- The settlement oracle path is implementation-ready.
- Feed configuration and validation rules are documented.
- Remaining Pyth work is reduced to straightforward coding tasks.
close_reason: Completed

title: Write risks and limitations note
description:
Write a short risks/limitations section as required by the project requirements.

Acceptance criteria:
- A risks/limitations section exists in the README or a dedicated file.
- Covers at minimum: oracle dependency and failure modes, devnet vs mainnet differences, position constraint is UI-only, Phoenix CLOB dependency, settlement timing approximation (5-min Pyth update interval), and no regulatory or compliance claims.
- Concise and factual — no speculation or legal language.

Done when:
- The note exists and is linked from the README.

title: Define core account model and on-chain invariants
description:
Translate the requirements into a concrete Anchor program state model before feature implementation.

Scope:
- Define config, market, vault, mint, and authority accounts.
- Define PDA strategy and seed conventions.
- Define settlement states and allowed transitions.
- Specify mint, merge, settle, redeem, and pause invariants in implementation terms.
- Clarify what must be enforced on-chain versus only in the frontend.

Done when:
- The program account graph is stable enough to implement against.
- The core invariants are written in a form that can drive tests.
- There is no ambiguity about the contract boundaries for the first implementation pass.
close_reason: Completed

title: Story: implement the trading screen and intent mapping
description:
Implement the main trade screen around one book and four user intents.

Acceptance criteria:
- The trade screen shows one Yes/USDC order book with Yes and No perspectives.
- Buy Yes, Buy No, Sell Yes, and Sell No map to the correct backend flows.
- Position-aware UI restrictions and guidance are shown consistently.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing frontend integration tests first for intent-to-action mapping, order-book rendering, and position-aware control states.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The trade screen shows one Yes/USDC order book with Yes and No perspectives.
2. No-side prices and actions are derived from the Yes book rather than a separate No market.
3. Buy Yes, Buy No, Sell Yes, and Sell No map to the correct backend flows.
4. Position-aware UI restrictions and guidance are shown consistently while Phoenix seat and bootstrap mechanics remain hidden from end users.
5. A countdown timer to 4:00 PM ET market close is visible during active trading.
6. A payoff display shows "You pay $X. You win $1.00 if [STOCK] closes above [STRIKE]."
notes:
Primary frontend behavior tests for this story:
- One Yes/USDC order book is rendered with both Yes and No perspectives.
- No-side pricing and actions are derived from the Yes book rather than a separate No market.
- Buy Yes, Buy No, Sell Yes, and Sell No map to the correct backend flows.
- Position-aware control states and guidance are enforced consistently while Phoenix mechanics stay hidden from end users.

title: Meridian foundation: architecture freeze and build scaffolding
description:
Goal: establish the technical and product foundation required before full feature implementation for Meridian.

First steps:
1. Freeze the core architecture and assumptions from the requirements: Solana devnet, Anchor, Phoenix, Pyth, settlement source-of-truth, 4:00 PM ET lifecycle behavior, merge requirement for Sell No, strike rounding/deduplication, admin authority model, and MVP demo scope.
2. Bootstrap the workspace and developer tooling: Anchor program workspace, TypeScript packages for frontend and automation, shared config, .env.example, reproducible dev/test commands, and deployment script skeletons.
3. Define the protocol state model and invariants in implementation terms: config account, strike market account, vault, Yes/No mints, settlement state machine, merge flow, redemption flow, and explicit on-chain invariant checks.
4. Prove critical external integrations with focused spikes: Phoenix market creation and seat workflow, Phoenix CPI for atomic Buy No and Sell No close path, Pyth pull-update settlement read, and devnet USDC handling.
5. Establish deterministic market operations: strike-generation module, idempotent daily market creation plan, automation job boundaries, and failure/override paths.
6. Create the initial testing baseline: invariant tests, settlement edge cases, and one happy-path end-to-end flow target for create -> mint -> trade -> settle -> redeem.

Done when:
- Architecture decisions and assumptions are documented and internally consistent with the requirements.
- The repo has a runnable scaffold for program, frontend, and automation work.
- The protocol state model is clear enough to begin implementation without re-litigating core flows.
- Phoenix and Pyth feasibility risks have been reduced to concrete implementation tasks.
- Follow-on child issues can be created directly from this epic without further discovery.

title: Establish initial invariant and end-to-end test plan
description:
Define the first testing slice needed to build safely from the start.

Scope:
- Define invariant tests for vault collateralization and payout conservation.
- Define settlement edge cases: above strike, below strike, exactly at strike, stale oracle, and wide confidence.
- Define the first happy-path integration target: create -> mint -> trade -> settle -> redeem.
- Identify which parts run locally versus on devnet.
- Keep the first pass narrow enough to support rapid implementation.

Done when:
- The first implementation tasks know what tests they are expected to satisfy.
- The project has a clear minimum quality bar before expanding scope.
- Full-lifecycle testing is broken into achievable milestones.
notes:
Test plan matrix to define in this task:
- Protocol integration tests: br-36c create-market account initialization and duplicate rejection; br-1xh mint/merge/pause invariants; br-15f oracle validation; br-3ic settlement transitions; br-1ml redemption.
- Trading integration tests: br-1f4 Phoenix bootstrap and seat workflow; br-3dw direct Buy Yes / Sell Yes; br-27j Buy No and Sell No composition.
- Automation integration tests: br-zt7 strike generation; br-399 morning creation job; br-2l5 settlement job retry/escalation.
- Narrow E2E smoke paths for br-2yp: create -> mint -> buy yes -> settle -> redeem; create -> mint -> buy no -> settle -> redeem; sell no via buy-yes + merge; morning creation -> afternoon settlement.
- Multi-user integration test: one user mints and quotes, another takes, both redeem correctly.
close_reason: done

title: Story: implement reproducible devnet bootstrap and environment setup
description:
Implement the minimum setup path needed to run Meridian reproducibly.

Acceptance criteria:
- A developer can install dependencies, configure env vars, and target devnet consistently.
- Required keys, program IDs, and network settings are explicit.
- Setup failures fail early with useful guidance.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing setup checks or smoke scripts first for missing env, wrong cluster config, and successful bootstrap.
close_reason: Completed

title: Story: implement strike generation and deduplication
description:
Implement the deterministic morning strike-generation logic.

Acceptance criteria:
- Strikes are generated from previous close using the agreed offsets.
- Rounded duplicates are removed deterministically.
- Output is stable for the same input market data.

TDD starting point:
- Write failing pure-function tests first for normal prices, low-priced duplicate cases, and deterministic output ordering.
notes:
Primary pure-function tests for this story, once br-2cf fixes the exact policy:
- Standard higher-priced input generates the expected +/-3/6/9 percent rounded strikes.
- Low-priced inputs that round to the same $10 bucket are deduplicated deterministically.
- Output ordering is stable for the same input.
- The chosen rule for including or omitting the rounded previous-close strike is enforced consistently.
close_reason: Already implemented: generateStrikes with dedup, 8 passing tests
